note: Curated from Wikipedia (2026 Iran war), Al Jazeera, CNN, NPR and Bloomberg reporting. Dates use local reporting conventions.

events.label: January 2026
events.title: Pre-war crisis deepens
events.body: Iranian security forces violently suppress anti-government protests while US–Iran talks over alleged nuclear weapons development stall. Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu lobbies Washington for military intervention.

events.label: 27 February
events.title: Operation Epic Fury authorized
events.body: President Trump orders coordinated US–Israeli strikes on Iran to commence, signing the order at 3:38 p.m. EST.

events.label: 28 February
events.title: War begins — Khamenei killed
events.body: The largest Israeli combat sortie in history hits 500 targets with over 1,200 bombs in 24 hours. Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei is killed in a decapitation strike. A strike on a Minab girls' school kills roughly 180. Iran retaliates with 170+ ballistic missiles against Israel, Gulf capitals, and the US Fifth Fleet HQ in Bahrain.

events.label: 1 March
events.title: Tehran pounded; Israel hit
events.body: A second strike wave levels Tehran's Revolutionary Court and internal-security headquarters. An Iranian missile breaches defenses and hits a Beit Shemesh synagogue, killing nine. Ali Larijani announces an interim leadership council.

events.label: 2 March
events.title: Nuclear sites struck; Lebanon front opens
events.body: US–Israeli forces strike Iranian nuclear facilities including Natanz. Hezbollah launches rockets into northern Israel, triggering the 2026 Lebanon war.

events.label: 3 March
events.title: Nine countries drawn in
events.body: 104 strikes hit 19 Iranian provinces in a single day. US naval forces sink the corvette IRIS Shahid Soleimani and submarine IRIS Fateh. Mojtaba Khamenei is elected Supreme Leader, confirmed 11 March.

events.label: Late March
events.title: Iran closes the Strait of Hormuz
events.body: Iranian forces block the world's most critical oil chokepoint, triggering the largest supply disruption in the history of the global oil market.

events.label: Late March – April
events.title: Ground war in Lebanon
events.body: Israeli ground operations push into southern Lebanon, with major battles at Khiam and Bint Jbeil. More than one-sixth of Lebanon's population is displaced.

events.label: 8 April
events.title: Fragile two-week ceasefire
events.body: A ceasefire takes effect but is strained immediately by continued Israeli operations in Lebanon.

events.label: 12–13 April
events.title: Islamabad Talks collapse
events.body: Negotiations in Pakistan fail. The US imposes a naval blockade of Iran while Iran keeps Hormuz shut — a 'dual blockade' strangling Gulf shipping.

events.label: 14 June
events.title: Memorandum to end the war announced
events.body: After weeks of fighting and back-channel talks, the parties announce an agreement framework to end hostilities.

events.label: 17 June
events.title: Signed at Versailles and Tehran
events.body: Trump signs the memorandum at the Palace of Versailles; President Masoud Pezeshkian signs in Tehran. The dual blockade is lifted the next day.

events.label: 6–7 July
events.title: Hormuz attacks resume
events.body: Iran fires on multiple commercial vessels in the Strait of Hormuz as ceasefire terms fray on both sides.

events.label: 8 July
events.title: Ceasefire collapses
events.body: The memorandum breaks down after attacks by both sides. Trump declares the deal 'over'; US strikes resume against Sirik, Qeshm and Bandar Abbas.

events.label: 12 July
events.title: IRGC declares Hormuz closed
events.body: The Revolutionary Guard declares the Strait of Hormuz closed over 'US interference', calling the strait Iran's 'inviolable red line'.

events.label: 15 July
events.title: US strikes Greater Tunb; blockade enforced
events.body: A 90-minute US operation hits missile storage and launch sites on Greater Tunb Island, then air-defense, drone and coastal-surveillance sites. A US aircraft disables a tanker attempting to run the blockade.

events.label: 16 July
events.title: Sixth straight day of exchanges
events.body: US and Iranian forces trade fire for a sixth consecutive day. Iran condemns a strike near a children's cancer hospital as 'barbaric'; the release of an American detainee is read by some as a possible opening for renewed talks.

events.label: 17 July
events.title: Strikes hit bridges and power grid; Marines board tanker
events.body: The US expands its air campaign to Iranian infrastructure, hitting five bridges in southern Iran and, per state agency IRNA, an airport and the power grid around Bandar Abbas — making good on Trump's threat to bomb bridges and power plants. Iran says seven were killed and warns the war 'will spread.' US Marines board the tanker M/T Wen Yao in the Gulf of Oman, escalating the blockade. Separately, Lebanon's government moves to end Hezbollah's military presence.

events.label: 17 July (evening)
events.title: War widens across the region
events.body: Iran broadens its retaliation beyond Israel and the US: missiles and drones strike US targets in Gulf states, a water desalination plant is hit in Kuwait, and Iranian accounts report attacks extended to Syria and Bahrain. The IRGC threatens 'more crushing' attacks on neighbors hosting US forces. The Wall Street Journal reports Trump is leaning toward expanding US operations, while a leaked US intelligence assessment concludes Iran retained about 70% of its missiles and launchers after the spring campaign. Satellite images reveal fresh damage inside the Bushehr nuclear power plant from strikes dated 7–12 July.

events.label: 18 July
events.title: Seventh night of strikes; Tehran rules out wartime talks
events.body: A seventh consecutive night of US strikes pushes deeper into Iran's interior and civilian infrastructure; CENTCOM says the aim is to keep degrading Iran's military, while Iran says an attack in the south cut water to roughly 10,000 people. Force posture escalates in step: the US deploys F-16s and F-35s to the Middle East and, per Axios, sends dozens of refueling tankers to Israel — giving shape to the expansion the WSJ reported Trump was weighing. An Iranian official declares negotiating during war 'over' and former IRGC chief Mohsen Rezaee threatens 'full-scale war', as UN Secretary-General António Guterres calls for a new diplomatic push after strikes on civilian areas.

events.label: 18 July (afternoon)
events.title: Iran suspends interim deal and widens strikes onto US allies
events.body: As the renewed war enters its second week, Iran says it is suspending its commitments to the interim deal with the US, reviving the dormant nuclear file, and warns that 'no political border' will be safe if strikes continue. It broadens retaliation onto US-aligned states: repeated strikes send plumes of smoke over Kuwait's Mangaf district and hit civilian sites, Jordan shoots down incoming missiles, and sirens sound in Bahrain. The US military says it has completed the latest round of strikes on bridges and water plants; Iranian outlets put the July death toll near 50. In Washington, Iraq signs 48 deals with US companies, including rebuilding an Iraq-Syria crude pipeline that could bypass the Strait of Hormuz.

events.label: 18–19 July
events.title: US troops killed in Jordan; eighth 'punishment' night of strikes
events.body: Iranian ballistic missiles strike Jordan's Muwaffaq Salti Airbase, killing two US service members and leaving one missing — the first American combat deaths since the war reignited on 8 July, and the crossing of the red line the White House had drawn against direct US casualties. CENTCOM says the troops were killed while partner forces defended against Iranian missile and drone attacks; Trump calls the deaths 'a sad thing.' Within hours the US strikes Iran for an eighth consecutive night, hitting the Sirik and Hajiabad areas of Hormozgan province in what NPR reports is framed as punishment for the deaths. Iran returns fire on US positions across the Gulf, saying it launched drones at the US military in Kuwait. Tehran hardens its rupture with Washington, calling off the interim agreement and threatening 'unforgettable lessons.'

events.label: 19 July (later)
events.title: War widens: Israel threatens re-entry, Kuwait plant ablaze
events.body: The renewed war visibly widens the day after the first US combat deaths. Israel warns it will respond 'with full force' if Iranian missiles strike its territory, threatening to reopen the front dormant since 8 July and turn the US-Iran duel back into a multi-front regional war. The US hits Iran for an eighth consecutive night to 'swiftly punish' the two soldiers killed at Jordan's Muwaffaq Salti Airbase, striking Sirik and Hajiabad in Hormozgan; Iran returns fire across the Gulf, setting a Kuwaiti electricity-and-water plant on fire — a second such strike in two days — with sirens in Bahrain and reportedly Jordan, and says US strikes hit an under-construction nuclear power plant. AP counts 16 US service-member deaths in the war overall, and The Economist reports the conflict is now the least popular US war since modern polling began. US Secretary of State Marco Rubio meets Lebanon's president in Washington as UN chief António Guterres presses for renewed diplomacy.

events.label: 19–20 July
events.title: War 'teeters on all-out'; another US death, ninth night 'in honour', oil tops $90
events.body: The renewed war slides toward all-out fighting. A US service member is killed and another wounded in Iraq during a 'controlled detonation' of a downed Iranian drone, a second theatre of American losses that ABC News says brings the war's US death toll to 17. President Trump then says a ninth consecutive night of US strikes — footage of which the military releases — was carried out 'in honour' of the fallen soldiers, recasting the campaign as open reprisal; the Washington Post reports the US now 'teeters on all-out war' and CNN warns it is 'threatening to spin out of control.' Crude passes $90 a barrel as Iran vows 'not a single drop' of oil or gas will pass the Strait of Hormuz while strikes continue, and Israel warns Iran's attacks are 'coming close' to its territory. Cutting the other way, US Secretary of State Marco Rubio signals openness to diplomacy even as the war escalates.
